claims.text: The Michigan Dogman myth went mainstream in 1987 when WTCM radio DJs Jack O'Malley and Steve Cook wrote a April Fool's prank song called 'The Legend' about a half-human half-dog hybrid that appears every ten years — and the prank backfired when listeners began calling in with their own dogman encounters going back over a hundred years.

evidence.quote:
or years but the
myth went mainstream in 1987. Jack O'Malley and Steve Cook worked at wtcm radio in Michigan as part of an April
Fool's Day prank they wrote a song called the legend the legend tells the story of a race of half human half dog
hybrids that appear every 10 years and stalk through the dark woods of Northern Michigan the song warned listeners that
these dogmen were due to appear this year and then the song ended ominously and somewhere in the North Woods
Darkness a creature walks up right the best advic

claims.rationale: The 1987 origin of 'The Legend' by Steve Cook (WTCM) is well-documented; the song was indeed written for an April Fool's Day broadcast and triggered an avalanche of listener calls reporting their own sightings, which Cook then began collecting and publishing. The song became a regional cultural touchstone in northern Michigan.

claims.text: Pre-1987 written records of dogman-like encounters in the Great Lakes region include 1887 lumberjacks who saw a 'large dog' stand up and screech, an 1857 letter describing a man-shaped beast with a gray wolf face, and an 1804 French fur trader's diary mentioning a 'loup-garou'.

evidence.quote:
the dog man the legend went back over a hundred years in 1887 Lumberjacks in the area came across what
they thought was a large dog until it stood up screeched at them and ran into the woods in 1857 a letter described a
frightening creature that stood up like a man that bore the face of a gray wolf a French fur Trader's diary was found
dated 1804 in it he described seeing a loop Guru the French word for werewolf even Native American leg

claims.rationale: These pre-1987 'archival' sightings have been promoted by Steve Cook on his Michigan Dogman archive and in subsequent paranormal coverage. Independent verification is thin — the 1804 fur-trader diary in particular has no clearly cited provenance. 'Loup-garou' (werewolf) folklore was indeed widespread in French-Canadian and Métis communities. The host presents these as documented but their actual evidentiary status ranges from genuine folklore to unsubstantiated retold story.

claims.text: In 1961 a night watchman at a Michigan factory near Haymarsh photographed a tall, hair-covered creature that alternately walked on four legs and stood upright in his driveway, taking the long-exposure shot with a Kodak Signet 35mm.

evidence.quote:
nesses would come forward and they would bring proof
in 1961 a night Watchman was working his shift at a factory near the haymarsh in Michigan one of the job perks was the
Watchman and his family could live on a house on the property the front porch of the house made it easy for him to keep an eye on things The Watchmen made his
rounds and headed back to the porch to sit relax and have a cup of coffee this was a remote area and the night was passing unevently the sound of crickets
and night birds filled the air then just after 3 A.M The Crickets and birds suddenly stopped there was only silence it was so abrupt that the Watchmen
started to feel uneasy then at the edge of the property he heard the distinctive sound of a chain-link fence rattling at
this late hour in such a remote location t

claims.rationale: The 'night watchman 1961' story is a piece of Michigan Dogman lore that has circulated through the Steve Cook material and various cryptid podcasts. The photograph itself is grainy and indistinct — typical of the genre — and there is no chain of custody / forensic analysis that elevates it above many similar bigfoot/cryptid photos. The host's framing as 'proof' is overstated.

claims.text: An anonymous Michigan politician driving Cinder Road outside Beulah in 2007 photographed a wolf-like print near a dead deer that, with a shotgun shell for scale, measures roughly 10-12 inches long — far larger than any known North American wolf print.

evidence.quote:
d be a giant stuffed animal someone playing a joke it wasn't a joke was it nope before he could finish his next
thought the creature dropped down on all four legs sprinted across the road and disappeared into the woods the man was frozen in his seat for a full minute
before he sung the courage to investigate he grabbed his flashlight and approached the dead deer there was no way he was going to follow the thing
into the woods but he wanted to see if it left any tracks it did the man was familiar with animal tracks and said it looked like a wolf print but was bigger
than any wolf print he'd ever seen luckily he had his digital camera with him he dropped the shotgun shell in the print for size reference a shotgun shell
is between two and three inches long depending on the gauge and the load so this footprint could be 10 or 12 inches long maybe bigger when interviewed the
man said he was familiar with the song on the radio and the local Le

claims.rationale: A typical gray wolf forefoot print is ~4-5 inches long; cougar prints up to ~4 inches; large dogs ~3-4 inches. A 10-12 inch print would be extraordinary if real. The photo and story are part of the Cook archive and are presented anonymously; with no chain-of-custody, no lab analysis, and only one witness, the evidentiary weight is low. The print could plausibly be a misidentified bear print, a doctored impression, or an outright fabrication.

claims.text: Wisconsin's parallel cryptid — the 'Beast of Bray Road' — is a 6-7 foot wolf-headed humanoid sighted by Walworth County residents since the 1990s; reporter Linda Godfrey began as a skeptic but later wrote multiple books defending the witnesses' sincerity.

evidence.quote:
's awfully
similar to the Michigan dog man and it's called The Beast of Bray Road in the 1990s residents of Walworth County reported seeing a humanoid werewolf
stalking a road outside of town it's described as being between six and seven feet tall covered in dark fur with a
head resembling a wolf or a bear and it's been seen moving on two legs and on four the creature was covered in newspapers in books documentaries even
TV shows there were so many reports about the Beast that local reporter Linda Godfrey was assigned to cover the
story now at

claims.rationale: The Beast of Bray Road is a real and well-documented localized cryptid phenomenon centered on Bray Road outside Elkhorn, Wisconsin. Linda Godfrey is a real journalist who covered the story for the Walworth County Week in the early 1990s and went on to publish multiple books (including 'The Beast of Bray Road' (2003) and 'Real Wolfmen'). The witness reports themselves are sincere; whether the creature is real is a separate question.

claims.text: On reflection, the famous 'Gable Film' — discovered at a 2007 yard sale and showing a creature attacking the cameraman — is a hoax: it was created by filmmaker Mike Agrusa as a viral promotion for Steve Cook's 'The Legend' song.

evidence comes from the Gable film The Gable film was discovered in 2007 in a box of junk that was sold at a yard sale it looks like it shot on eight
millimeter film sometime in the 70s it's the typical home movie kids playing in the snow dad chopping wood fixing his
car then the film takes an ominous turn there are shots of the photographer scanning the woods for something though it's difficult to see there are shots of
the dog who seems excited by something and appears to have caught a scent then we're back in the woods a photographer scanning the trees again
and then stops on this it seems to be an animal on all fours but I can't tell what kind of animal
then it starts walking then it starts running the photographer takes off an

ut once again the only drug found in his system
was a small amount of THC later Austin would recount what happened but his Story made no sense it involved demons
and witches and dark figures in the shadows Philip Resnick a psychiatrist was brought in to assess Austin's mental
state in his 38-page report Resnick believed Austin was suffering from Clinical lycanthropy in other words he
thought he was a werewolf stories about dog men and werewolves exist in every culture and go back to
the beginnings of those cultures and it's easy to see why there are actual physical and mental conditions that can
make humans look and act like wolves but what about the sightings unfortunately the only photos we have are blurry and
most of them are hoaxes the Gable film is amazing but that's also a hoax when Mike agrusa heard Steve Cook's song The
Legend he thought it would be fun to create a mini horror fi

claims.rationale: The host's own debunking is essentially correct. Mike Agrusa publicly confirmed in 2010 that he created the Gable Film as a fan-made promotional piece for Cook's Legend franchise — Cook himself confirmed it as well. The film is artful and effective but is a verified hoax.

claims.text: Some 'wolfman' sightings can be explained by hypertrichosis, a real genetic condition causing hair to grow over the entire body except palms and soles — exemplified by 16th-century courtier Petrus Gonsalvus, whose marriage may have inspired Beauty and the Beast.

evidence.quote:
sidered
freaks they were really just regular people with a condition called hypertrichosis hypertrichosis is a condition usually caused by a genetic
mutation that causes hair to grow all over the body I've got a cousin with a unibrow different affliction
typically hair grows everywhere but not on the palms of the hands or soles of the feet so a person really can look like an actual Wolf Man wolf person
don't do that we signal anymore nah that's that's going away no thank God it was getting old the
first recorded case of hypertrichosis is Petrus Gonzalez he was act

claims.rationale: Hypertrichosis universalis is a real condition; the palmar/plantar exception is generally accurate. Petrus Gonsalvus (Pedro Gonzales) was a real 16th-century Canary Islands native presented at the French court of Henri II, and several of his children also had the condition. The Gonsalvus / Beauty-and-the-Beast connection is a real scholarly hypothesis (notably advanced by Roberto Zapperi) tying his marriage to Catherine to Villeneuve's 1740 fairy tale, though it's speculative rather than definitively established.

claims.text: The word 'lycanthropy' derives from the Greek myth of King Lycaon of Arcadia, who tested Zeus by serving him the flesh of his own son and was punished by being transformed into a wolf — the founding myth of the werewolf trope.

evidence.quote:
Music]
the word lycanthropy comes from King lichen of Arcadia in one telling of the legend Lycan wanted to test if Zeus was
really an all-knowing God so lichen served Zeus the Flesh of lichen's own son Zeus was all knowing and he wasn't
pleased with the meal no no did you feel like one of those comment Cuts nope Zeus killed all of lichen's children all 49
of them the boy that was sacrificed was brought back to life and lichen was transformed into a wolf whoa that's worse than a one star review on GrubHub
way worse clinical lycanthropy is the condition where a human believes that they are part

claims.rationale: True. The etymology of 'lycanthropy' from Lykáōn (Lycaon) is correct. The Lycaon myth (recounted by Ovid in Metamorphoses Book I and by Pausanias) is the canonical Greek origin story of werewolves. The detail of '49 children' varies by source — some accounts give Lycaon 50 sons (the Lycaonidae), so the host is broadly faithful to the tradition. The transcription artifact 'lichen' for 'Lycaon' is a speech-recognition error.

claims.text: In the 2012 'Miami zombie attack', Rudy Eugene stripped naked and gnawed off ~75% of homeless man Ronald Poppo's face over 18 minutes before being shot dead by police — and toxicology found only trace THC, not the bath salts initially blamed.

evidence.quote:
uld be awful in 2012 Rudy Eugene was cruising the back streets of Miami at some point he gets
out of his car and attacks a homeless man named Ronald Papo Eugene strips off all of his clothes and starts beating
Papo until he can't move Eugene then jumps on popo and begins eating his face and this goes on for 18 minutes before
police respond now the entire event is covered by a security camera and I won't put that video here but if you really
want to see it you don't I'll put a link below so when police finally arrive they command Rudy Eugene to get off poppo but
all he does is turn around and snarl police shoot him five times and he dies at the scene and the aftermath is
gruesome 75 percent o

claims.rationale: All confirmed: the May 26, 2012 attack on Ronald Poppo by Rudy Eugene under the MacArthur Causeway in Miami was widely reported. Police shot Eugene dead. Poppo survived but lost most of his face. The Miami-Dade Medical Examiner's toxicology report found only marijuana metabolites, contradicting initial media speculation about bath salts (cathinones).

claims.rationale: Confirmed: the August 15, 2016 Tequesta, Florida attack by 19-year-old Austin Harouff on John and Michelle Stevens, with neighbor Jeff Fisher being knife-wounded, is well documented. Toxicology reportedly found minimal substance use. Forensic psychiatrist Phillip Resnick did testify that Harouff suffered from clinical lycanthropy / paranoid schizophrenia at the time of the attack, and Harouff was found not guilty by reason of insanity in 2022. Date '1987' in entities list is incidental — Harouff was born around 1997.

claims.text: On reflection, despite acknowledging that most dogman/werewolf evidence is blurry photos and outright hoaxes, the host has shifted from skeptic to provisional believer — moved by the sincerity of journalists like Linda Godfrey and the willingness of sheriffs and deputies to file official reports about wolfmen.

evidence.quote:
take the
word of eyewitnesses who often seem questionable trust me bro right but when journalists like Linda Godfrey go from
Cryptid skeptic to Cryptid believer I reevaluate my position and when sheriffs and deputies put their careers and
reputations on the line by filling out official reports about werewolves I re-evaluate my position I still think
most of these stories are mistakes or false memories or flat-out hoaxes but not all of them can be explained and for
me personally that's my favorite way to end an episode of the y-files is by reevaluating my position being ready to
believe something new so if you're that night Watchman or that farmer or small town Deputy who has a picture on his phone that you can't explain and you're
afraid to send it anywhere send it to me I'm ready to believe
[Music]
thank you so much for hanging out with me today my name is

claims.rationale: This is the host's explicit verdict-section position: most stories are mistakes/hoaxes, but the witness-quality argument (sincere insiders with reputations on the line) leaves him ready to believe. directTruth omitted because the underlying meta-claim ('the witnesses' sincerity is itself evidence') is more an epistemic stance than an empirical proposition; serious epistemologists would push back that sincere conviction is famously unreliable when reality-testing extraordinary claims.

dependencies.rationale: Acknowledges the Gable Film hoax while still maintaining residual openness to the phenomenon.
